title: Vehicle registration plates of Germany

infobox.value: ([German](/wiki/German_language "German language"): *Kraftfahrzeug-Kennzeichen* or, more colloquially, *Nummernschilder*) are mandatory [alphanumeric plates](/wiki/Number_plates "Number plates") used to display the *registration mark* of a vehicle registered in [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany"). They have existed in the country since 1906, with the current system in use since 1956. German registration plates are alphanumeric plates in a standardised format, issued officially by the district authorities.[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)
for *Deutschland* = Germany) and the [Flag of Europe](/wiki/Flag_of_Europe "Flag of Europe") (12 golden stars forming a circle on a blue background).

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/97/Zwickau_license_plate.JPG/250px-Zwickau_license_plate.JPG)](/wiki/File:Zwickau_license_plate.JPG)

1-letter area code Z for [Zwickau](/wiki/Zwickau "Zwickau")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/30/Deutsches_KFZ-Kennzeichen_TR-XB32.jpg/250px-Deutsches_KFZ-Kennzeichen_TR-XB32.jpg)](/wiki/File:Deutsches_KFZ-Kennzeichen_TR-XB32.jpg)

2-letter area code TR for [Trier](/wiki/Trier "Trier")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9b/Germany_Cuxhaven_license_plate_Lower_Saxony.jpg/250px-Germany_Cuxhaven_license_plate_Lower_Saxony.jpg)](/wiki/File:Germany_Cuxhaven_license_plate_Lower_Saxony.jpg)

3-letter area code CUX for [Cuxhaven](/wiki/Cuxhaven "Cuxhaven")

**Vehicle registration plates** ([German](/wiki/German_language "German language"): *Kraftfahrzeug-Kennzeichen* or, more colloquially, *Nummernschilder*) are mandatory [alphanumeric plates](/wiki/Number_plates "Number plates") used to display the *registration mark* of a vehicle registered in [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany"). They have existed in the country since 1906, with the current system in use since 1956. German registration plates are alphanumeric plates in a standardised format, issued officially by the district authorities.[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)

All motorised vehicles participating in road traffic on public space, whether moving or stationary, have to bear the plates allotted to them, displayed at the appropriate spaces at the front and rear. Additionally, the official seals on the plates show their validity which can also be proven by the documentation coming with them. Motorcycles and trailers carry only a rear plate.

A significant feature of German vehicle registration plates is the area code, which can be used to tell the district of registration. It has developed into a widespread habit in Germany, even a children's game when travelling, to guess "where that vehicle is from".[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)

## Format and legal requirements

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Germany&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Format and legal requirements")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/39/Licenceplate_of_Germany_Kreis_Steinburg.JPG/330px-Licenceplate_of_Germany_Kreis_Steinburg.JPG)](/wiki/File:Licenceplate_of_Germany_Kreis_Steinburg.JPG)

Number plate in post-1994 format (FE-style)[[a]](#cite_note-5)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/93/Plate-KA-RR232.JPG/250px-Plate-KA-RR232.JPG)](/wiki/File:Plate-KA-RR232.JPG)

Number plate in pre-1994 format (DIN-style), no longer issued but still in use[[b]](#cite_note-6)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d0/German_Licence_Plate_N-M4.jpg/250px-German_Licence_Plate_N-M4.jpg)](/wiki/File:German_Licence_Plate_N-M4.jpg)

Number plate with few characters, hence shorter than standard 520×110

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ad/GERMANY%2C_Osterode_Am_Herz%2C_EEC_motorcycle_plate%2C_large_size_-_Flickr_-_woody1778a.jpg/120px-GERMANY%2C_Osterode_Am_Herz%2C_EEC_motorcycle_plate%2C_large_size_-_Flickr_-_woody1778a.jpg)](/wiki/File:GERMANY,_Osterode_Am_Herz,_EEC_motorcycle_plate,_large_size_-_Flickr_-_woody1778a.jpg)

### Legal

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Germany&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Legal")]

The legal requirements for these licence plates are laid down in a federal law titled *Verordnung über die Zulassung von Fahrzeugen zum Straßenverkehr* (Ordinance on the admission of vehicles for road traffic), or in the shortened version *Fahrzeug-Zulassungs-Verordnung, FZV*[[5]](#cite_note-FZV-10) which replaced part of an older law named *Straßenverkehrszulassungsordnung, StVZO*[[f]](#cite_note-11) in 2011. The law distinguishes between *Kennzeichen* meaning a specific combination of letters and digits, and *Kennzeichenschilder* which are the physical licence plates.[[5]](#cite_note-FZV-10): §12 In everyday language, these terms are often replaced indifferently by *Nummernschild* and rarely is the difference emphasized by restricted use of either *Nummer* or *Schild*.

### Physical characteristics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Germany&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Physical characteristics")]

German number plates are rectangular, with standard dimensions 520 mm × 110 mm (20+1⁄2 in × 4+3⁄8 in) for cars, trucks, buses and their trailers. Plates bearing few characters may have reduced length but must retain the size and shape of the characters. Plates in two lines for the said types of vehicles[[g]](#cite_note-12) measure 340 mm × 200 mm (13+3⁄8 in × 7+7⁄8 in). Motorcycles also have plates in two lines but with specific dimensions: Plates for "large motorcycles" (engine displacement over 125 cc or power output exceeding 11 kW (15 bhp)) issued until 2011 combine a size of 280 mm × 200 mm (11 in × 7+7⁄8 in) with characters of the same size as used for car plates, while those issued since 2011 have characters of reduced size and measure either 180 mm × 200 mm (7+1⁄8 in × 7+7⁄8 in) or 220 mm × 200 mm (8+5⁄8 in × 7+7⁄8 in). Plates for "light motorcycles" (*Leichtkrafträder* up to 125 cc and 11 kW (15 bhp)) combine a size of 255 mm × 130 mm (10 in × 5+1⁄8 in) with characters of reduced size. This smallest size of plates is also used for agricultural tractors with a maximum speed of 40 km/h (25 mph) and their trailers and may also be issued as an exception for certain import cars, when a plate of regular size cannot be applied at the available space.

The characters on the licence plate, as well as the narrow rim framing it, are black on a white background.[[5]](#cite_note-FZV-10): §12(1) In standard size they are 75 mm (3 in) high, and 47.5 mm (1+7⁄8 in) wide for letters or 44.5 mm (1+3⁄4 in) wide for digits. The smaller plates bear characters of 49 mm (1+7⁄8 in) height and 31 or 29 mm (1+1⁄4 or 1+1⁄8 in) width, respectively.[[5]](#cite_note-FZV-10): Appendix 4 In the current system, introduced in 1956, they consist of an area code of one, two or three letters, followed by an identifier sequence of one or two letters and one to four digits. The total quantity of characters on the plate must not exceed eight. Identifiers consisting of one letter with one- or two-digit numbers are often reserved for motorcycle use since there is less space for plates on these vehicles, especially before the reduction of plate size to 180 × 200 mm and 220 × 200 mm in 2011.[[6]](#cite_note-13)

#### Typeface

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Germany&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Typeface")]

Modern German plates use a typeface called [FE-Schrift](/wiki/FE-Schrift "FE-Schrift") (*fälschungserschwerende Schrift*, tamper-hindering script). It is designed so that the letter *P* cannot be altered to look exactly like an *R*, and vice versa; nor can the *F* or the *L* be forged to equal an *E*, etc. Another feature is the equal width of all characters, different from the old [DIN 1451](/wiki/DIN_1451 "DIN 1451") script which had been in use since the introduction of the current system in 1956. FE-Schrift can be read by [OCR](/wiki/Optical_character_recognition "Optical character recognition") software for [automatic number plate recognition](/wiki/Automatic_number_plate_recognition "Automatic number plate recognition") more easily than DIN 1451.

The present style was introduced in 1994 and became mandatory in 2000, so the number of licence plates issued in the old style has become very rare. As with many plates for countries within the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union"), a blue strip on the left shows a shortened [country code](/wiki/List_of_international_vehicle_registration_codes "List of international vehicle registration codes") in white text (**D** for *Deutschland* = Germany) and the [Flag of Europe](/wiki/Flag_of_Europe "Flag of Europe") (12 golden stars forming a circle on a blue background).

#### Material

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Germany&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Material")]

Traditionally, German licence plates are made of aluminium sheeting, with embossed characters rising above background level. Over the years, however, various other materials have been used, albeit to a small extent only. Since 2013, a new style of plates made of plastic is allowed.[[7]](#cite_note-14) They are said to be less sensitive to mechanical damage and to cause less CO2 emission in their production, but are more expensive.[[8]](#cite_note-15)

Number plates are retroflective, but must not be mirroring, concealed or soiled, nor may they be covered by glass, foil or similar layers.[[5]](#cite_note-FZV-10): §12(2) Occasionally, drivers who adorn their licence plate with a badge of their favourite football club are fined and ordered to restore the original state.[[9]](#cite_note-16)[[10]](#cite_note-17)

## Constituent elements

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Germany&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Constituent elements")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b0/Kfz-Kennzeichen_in_Deutschland.svg/330px-Kfz-Kennzeichen_in_Deutschland.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Kfz-Kennzeichen_in_Deutschland.svg)

Map of German districts and their licence plate codes

### Area code

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Germany&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Area code")]

The first part or *Unterscheidungszeichen* consists of one, two or three letters representing the district where the car was registered, such as **B** for [Berlin](/wiki/Berlin "Berlin") or **HSK** for [Hochsauerlandkreis](/wiki/Hochsauerlandkreis "Hochsauerlandkreis"). These letters basically refer to the [German districts](/wiki/Districts_of_Germany "Districts of Germany"), yet after some changes in the law they are no longer unambiguous. When the current system was introduced, each district was assigned exactly one abbreviation. Whenever a district was merged with another or changed its name, their area code would be redefined. Whereas existing registrations would remain valid, any vehicle registered henceforth could only be issued the current code.[[example 1]](#cite_note-18) In some cases an [urban district](/wiki/Urban_districts_of_Germany "Urban districts of Germany") and the surrounding non-urban district share the same letter code. Usually, these are distinguished by different letter/digit patterns.[[example 2]](#cite_note-19) The assignment of each code and combination is registered with the *Zentrales Fahrzeugregister* (ZFZR).[[11]](#cite_note-ZFZR-20): §34

Since 2013, however, area codes long abolished were re-introduced,[[12]](#cite_note-IKL-21)[[13]](#cite_note-22) revoking the rule of unambiguity. In consequence, many districts now use more than one code,[[example 3]](#cite_note-23) and certain codes, on the other hand, are not assigned to one district only.[[example 4]](#cite_note-24) Likewise, several cities that share their code with the surrounding rural[[h]](#cite_note-25) districts have started using any available codes for both districts without any distinction; the city of [Regensburg](/wiki/Regensburg "Regensburg"), for example, and the surrounding [rural district of Regensburg](/wiki/Regensburg_(district) "Regensburg (district)") used different systems with their code **R** only until 2007.

Germany includes diacritical marks in the letters of some codes, that is the letters Ä,[[14]](#cite_note-26) Ö, and Ü,[[i]](#cite_note-27) but not [ẞ](/wiki/%C3%9F "ß").[[j]](#cite_note-28) For a long time, German codes kept to the rule that a code with an umlaut would prohibit another code with the respective blank vowel, e.g. there could not be a district code **FU** as the code **FÜ** was already in use for [Fürth](/wiki/F%C3%BCrth "Fürth"). This rule was disregarded in 1996, when **BÖ** was introduced for [Bördekreis](/wiki/B%C3%B6rdekreis "Bördekreis") in spite of **BO** existing for [Bochum](/wiki/Bochum "Bochum"). However Bördekreis got BK in 2007, probably because of confusions abroad, for fines etc.[[k]](#cite_note-29)

#### Development of the area code

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Germany&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Development of the area code")]

When the area codes were introduced, they were intended mainly as a means for police officers to identify speeders and other traffic offenders. However, they soon developed into everyday abbreviations of people's home areas and were cherished or despised. Sometimes, the codes of neighbouring districts were given mocking or spiteful meanings.[[example 5]](#cite_note-30) When districts merged and only one of their codes could be continued, fierce battles might ensue about which one.[[15]](#cite_note-31)

#### The Büsingen exclave

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Germany&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: The Büsingen exclave")]

Main article: [Büsingen am Hochrhein](/wiki/B%C3%BCsingen_am_Hochrhein "Büsingen am Hochrhein")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9a/WEST_GERMANY%2C_BUSINGEN_German_Enclave_in_Switzerland_1970%27s_passenger_plate_-_Flickr_-_woody1778a.jpg/250px-WEST_GERMANY%2C_BUSINGEN_German_Enclave_in_Switzerland_1970%27s_passenger_plate_-_Flickr_-_woody1778a.jpg)](/wiki/File:WEST_GERMANY,_BUSINGEN_German_Enclave_in_Switzerland_1970%27s_passenger_plate_-_Flickr_-_woody1778a.jpg)

Licence plate from the municipality of Büsingen, 1970s

Since 1968, a peculiar rule has applied to the municipality of *Büsingen am Hochrhein* which is a German [exclave](/wiki/Exclave "Exclave") completely surrounded by [Swiss](/wiki/Switzerland "Switzerland") territory. Although Büsingen belongs to the German district of [Konstanz](/wiki/Konstanz_(district) "Konstanz (district)"), it is part of the Swiss customs area. For this reason, a vehicle registered to a citizen of Büsingen does not bear **KN** for Konstanz but **BÜS**, signifying to Swiss customs officers that this is in fact a domestic vehicle. There are about 700 cars with this area code, which makes Büsingen the smallest and least-populated registration precinct in Germany.[[16]](#cite_note-32)

### Stickers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Germany&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Stickers")]

Registration plates become valid with the official seal of registration.[[5]](#cite_note-FZV-10): §12(3) This is a sticker of 45 mm (1+3⁄4 in) diameter, following the area code and bearing, in colours, the seal of the respective German *[Bundesland](/wiki/States_of_Germany "States of Germany")* with the name of the state and the issuing district authority added in print. Older stickers were monochrome, black on silver or white, and smaller (35 mm; 1+3⁄8 in), depicting the seal of either the Bundesland or the city district. Vehicles used by federal institutions, such as *[Bundespolizei](/wiki/Federal_Police_(Germany) "Federal Police (Germany)")*, carry the German *[Bundesadler](/wiki/Coat_of_arms_of_Germany "Coat of arms of Germany")* instead of a *Bundesland* seal.

The rear plate bears, above the official seal, the vehicle safety test sticker. This [test](/wiki/Vehicle_inspection#Germany "Vehicle inspection") is obligatory three years after the first registration, and every two years after that. (This time scheme differs for certain vehicles, such as buses, trucks, taxis, etc.) The expiration date can be seen at one glance, as the sticker is attached with the month of expiration pointing upwards. The black mark framing the sectors on either side of *12* thus makes it easy for the police to read the expiration month from a distance. Like the hand on a clock, the marking shows the position of a number on the face of a clock. The year when the next safety test is due is printed in the centre of the sticker and also indicated by the colour of the sticker which is repeated every six years.

Whereas the technical inspection was introduced in 1951, it was not before 1961 that a decal on the rear plate indicated when it was due.[[22]](#cite_note-38) The inspection generally had to be performed every two years, only later brand new cars were granted an extra year before their first technical inspection. Consequently, there were not so many different colours needed, and the pattern was different then.

Between 1985 and 2010, a similar yet hexagonal sticker was applied to the front plate, certifying the [emission test](/wiki/Vehicle_emissions_control "Vehicle emissions control") which had to be performed separately since March 1985. With a change of laws effective in 2010, the emission test was incorporated into the safety test, so the emission sticker became obsolete.[[23]](#cite_note-39)

Bus with repeater plate, due to the bike carrier

All these stickers are specially treated to be easily transferred onto the licence plates, but hard to be removed without damaging the plate itself, making them relatively counterfeit-proof.

The only licence plates which do not need to carry either seal are repeaters. These are obligatory when the original rear plate is covered, in part or whole, by cargo or attached parts, such as bicycle carriers.[[5]](#cite_note-FZV-10): §12(10)

### Serial letters and digits

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Germany&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Serial letters and digits")]

The final identifier or *Erkennungsnummer* of the licence plate consists of one or two letters, followed by a number of up to four digits. Thus, basically any combination from A1 to ZZ9999 is possible, yet restricted by the maximum length of eight characters, including the area code. All 26 letters of the Latin alphabet may be used, yet this was not always so. In order to avoid confusion between B and 8, F and E, G and 6, I and 1, O and Q and 0, those six letters were excluded from the middle part of registration plates. In 1992, the letters B, F and G were permitted, and in 2000 the alphabet was completed as I, O and Q have been allowed. In the very first months of the numbering system, between July and November 1956, the letter I was used but J was not. This was soon reversed, but single vintage cars kept sporting their letter I between 1956 and 2000 when it was re-introduced.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/65/Germany_plate_Hesse_FST_683.jpg/250px-Germany_plate_Hesse_FST_683.jpg)](/wiki/File:Germany_plate_Hesse_FST_683.jpg)

This plate from [Frankfurt am Main](/wiki/Frankfurt_am_Main "Frankfurt am Main") bears the letters F ST, whereas FS T may be found on a vehicle from [Freising](/wiki/Freising_(district) "Freising (district)").

In the style used until 1994, a hyphen following the area code was used to separate the two groups of letters. This no longer appears in the new format but is often retained, as the space between the geographic identifier and serial letters is a significant character and must be considered when writing down or transmitting a number. For example, **F ST 683** is different from **FS T 683**. The risk of confusion can be avoided by writing a hyphen after the city code, like **F-ST 683**. For this reason, the police will usually radio the location name and spell out the next letters, using the German [spelling alphabet](/wiki/Spelling_alphabet#Table_of_spelling_alphabets_by_language "Spelling alphabet"). Thus, **F ST 683** would be radioed as *[Frankfurt](/wiki/Frankfurt_am_Main "Frankfurt am Main"), Siegfried, Theodor, sechs-acht-drei* and **FS T 683** as *[Freising](/wiki/Freising_(district) "Freising (district)"), Theodor, sechs-acht-drei*. If the officer should not know the meaning of the area code, he would spell it out too, as *Friedrich, Siegfried, Trennung (separation), Theodor*, etc.

While the number is issued by each district authority separately, a probable split between two or more districts sharing the same area code has to be considered.[[example 6]](#cite_note-42) Further restrictions are caused by "prohibited" combinations (see below).[[n]](#cite_note-44)

#### Personalised plates

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Germany&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Personalised plates")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c9/Autokennzeichen_KI-EL_1_%28Kiel_32.902%29.jpg/250px-Autokennzeichen_KI-EL_1_%28Kiel_32.902%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Autokennzeichen_KI-EL_1_(Kiel_32.902).jpg)

Very old example of a personalised plate, from [Kiel](/wiki/Kiel "Kiel")

For an extra charge of €10.20, vehicle owners can register a personalised identifier, keeping to the above rules. In most cases of personalised plates, owners choose their initials and a number reflecting their date of birth. In this fashion, fictional Mrs *Ulrike Mustermann*, born 2 May 1965 and living in [Essen](/wiki/Essen "Essen"), might choose *E-UM 2565* for her car. By combining area code and random letters, further possibilities arise, such as a man from [Oldenburg](/wiki/Oldenburg_(city) "Oldenburg (city)") named Olaf, born on Christmas Eve, could choose *OL-AF 2412*. A resident of the town of [Pirna](/wiki/Pirna "Pirna") might choose *PIR-AT 77*, *Pirat* meaning "pirate" in German. [Kiel](/wiki/Kiel "Kiel") and [Kleve](/wiki/Kleve "Kleve") are two examples of places where the number plate can spell out all of the city name.

These [vanity plates](/wiki/Vanity_plate "Vanity plate") can only be made up of the available prefixes and numbers, within the general rules. A [James Bond](/wiki/James_Bond "James Bond") fan from Hamburg would not be allowed the plate *HH-JB 007* because leading digits 0 (or even [double-0](/wiki/00_Agent "00 Agent")) are not possible; however, he might strive for **HH-J 8007** or **HH-OO 7**, imitating digits by letters or vice versa. The owner of a [Volkswagen Polo](/wiki/Volkswagen_Polo "Volkswagen Polo") can certainly show *VW* in the middle section, but neither *PO-LO 1995* nor *VW-P0 L01* would be possible, as these prefixes are not issued nor may letters and digits be mixed at will. Nonetheless, a notable variety of personalised number plates can be spotted on German roads.

#### Company cars

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Germany&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Company cars")]

Whereas private persons are required to register their vehicle with the district authority of their residence, commercial enterprises can choose to establish branch offices from where to register at will – either for financial reasons, as insurance fees are dependent on the address of registration, or to obtain an interesting licence plate. On the other hand, other enterprises show their origin proudly nationwide. One of these is [ADAC](/wiki/ADAC "ADAC"), the German automobile association, based in Munich. All their assistance cars, operating from the Alps to the North Sea, bear an **M** plate.

[Deutsche Bahn](/wiki/Deutsche_Bahn "Deutsche Bahn"), after being [corporatised](/wiki/Corporatization "Corporatization") and relinquishing their *Behördenkennzeichen* (authority plate) **DB**, prefers this logo as their middle letters, e. g. **F-DB** for the Frankfurt office.
[Deutsche Telekom](/wiki/Deutsche_Telekom "Deutsche Telekom"), the largest telecommuncations company, often uses **BN** plates, as their headquarters remained in Bonn, where the company originated out of a governmental post & telephone agency. Yet wherever the respective branch office may be, the middle letters **DT** are preferred.

The [Kone](/wiki/Kone "Kone") company registers their German vehicles in [Koblenz](/wiki/Koblenz "Koblenz"), with middle letters NE, thus displaying their trademark on their plates. [BMW](/wiki/BMW "BMW"), owner of [Mini](/wiki/Mini_(marque) "Mini (marque)"), registers all Mini press/marketing cars in the district of [Minden-Lübbecke](/wiki/Minden-L%C3%BCbbecke "Minden-Lübbecke") which holds the code **MI**, to get "MI-NI" number plates for the cars. BMW itself is based in Munich, yet *M-INI* plates are not possible to issue, as three letters after the district code are not permitted.

From 1970s up to 1994, [Essen](/wiki/Essen "Essen") city buses owned by city transport company EVAG (Essener Verkehrs-AG) were registered with *E-AT* number plates. This may be regarded a pun, as *eat* translates into German as *Essen*.[[25]](#cite_note-45)[[26]](#cite_note-46)

### Prohibited combinations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Germany&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Prohibited combinations")]

Combinations that are regarded as a *Verstoß gegen die guten Sitten*, which means "offence to moral and customs", are disallowed or otherwise avoided.[[5]](#cite_note-FZV-10): §9(1) This refers mostly to abbreviations relating to [Nazi Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany"), such as *NS* ([National Socialism](/wiki/Nazism "Nazism")), *KZ* (*Konzentrationslager*, [concentration camp](/wiki/Internment "Internment")), *HJ* (*Hitlerjugend*, [Hitler Youth](/wiki/Hitler_Youth "Hitler Youth")), *SS* ([Schutzstaffel](/wiki/Schutzstaffel "Schutzstaffel")) and *SA* ([Sturmabteilung](/wiki/Sturmabteilung "Sturmabteilung")). Therefore, these two-letter combinations are generally not issued in any district, nor do the city districts of [Hanover](/wiki/Hanover "Hanover"), [Nuremberg](/wiki/Nuremberg "Nuremberg"), [Cologne](/wiki/Cologne "Cologne") and [Stuttgart](/wiki/Stuttgart "Stuttgart") issue one-letter plates which would result in the combinations H-J, N-S, K-Z, S-A, S-D and S-S.[[27]](#cite_note-47)[[28]](#cite_note-Köln-48)[[29]](#cite_note-Stuttgart-49)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/34/Stra%C3%9Fenwacht_vom_Allgemeinen_Deutschen_Automobil-Club_e.V._%28ADAC%29_%28Kiel_36.733%29.jpg/250px-Stra%C3%9Fenwacht_vom_Allgemeinen_Deutschen_Automobil-Club_e.V._%28ADAC%29_%28Kiel_36.733%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stra%C3%9Fenwacht_vom_Allgemeinen_Deutschen_Automobil-Club_e.V._(ADAC)_(Kiel_36.733).jpg)

ADAC car parade in 1965 – the plate on the far left reads M-SA 617

Those prefixes must not be issued as middle letters now, yet the list of forbidden codes appears to have developed over the decades. In the 1960s, the renowned [ADAC](/wiki/ADAC "ADAC") had no qualms with the middle letters *SA*, as seen on an archive photo. Although the "infamous" combinations were generally excluded from the list of possible area codes with the introduction of the current system, the French occupation force had between 1945 and 1949 used the combination **SA** followed by the double-digit numbers 01 to 08 for the then seven rural districts in the [Saar Protectorate](/wiki/Saar_(protectorate) "Saar (protectorate)") and its capital [Saarbrücken](/wiki/Saarbr%C3%BCcken "Saarbrücken"). By 1990, however, codes like these were not taken into consideration for newly formed districts in former GDR: The district *[Sächsische Schweiz](/wiki/S%C3%A4chsische_Schweiz "Sächsische Schweiz")* used the name of its capital, [Pirna](/wiki/Pirna "Pirna"), in its code **PIR**, to avoid the use of *SS*. When the districts of [Torgau](/wiki/Torgau "Torgau"), [Delitzsch](/wiki/Delitzsch "Delitzsch") and [Oschatz](/wiki/Oschatz "Oschatz") merged into *[Nordsachsen](/wiki/Nordsachsen "Nordsachsen")*, they combined their initials into **TDO**, instead of abbreviating *Northern Saxony* as *NS*.[[o]](#cite_note-50)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9e/Deutsches_Kfz-Kennzeichen_mit_Erkennungsnummer_NS.jpg/250px-Deutsches_Kfz-Kennzeichen_mit_Erkennungsnummer_NS.jpg)](/wiki/File:Deutsches_Kfz-Kennzeichen_mit_Erkennungsnummer_NS.jpg)

Example of banned combination ([NS](/wiki/Nazism "Nazism")) which was issued accidentally

On the other hand, the area codes **HH** and **AH** were chosen for *Hansestadt Hamburg* and former district *[Ahaus](/wiki/Ahaus "Ahaus")*, although they could be interpreted as *[Heil Hitler](/wiki/Nazi_salute "Nazi salute")* and *[Adolf Hitler](/wiki/Adolf_Hitler "Adolf Hitler")*, respectively. In everyday German, the letters AH are not regarded as an obvious abbreviation for that name,[[p]](#cite_note-51) even less so in the 1950s when the lists were created.[[q]](#cite_note-52) Nonetheless, these two-letter codes and the respective numerals 18 and 88, signifying the first and eighth letter of the alphabet, obviously have developed into [Nazi symbols](/wiki/Nazi_symbolism "Nazi symbolism"). They are therefore generally avoided in the serial part of licence plates, although they may be found sometimes.[[30]](#cite_note-53) Generally, the decision whether or not a certain combination is permitted lies with the respective district authority. In Brandenburg, any plates that are related to Hitler, the Hitler salute, etc. cannot be issued, especially if they would be bearing digits 1888, 8818, 8888 or ending in 88, 888, 188. Nor can the combinations AH 18 and HH 18 be issued to new owners.[[31]](#cite_note-54) Some districts however allow these combinations if they are the owner's initials (for example, *Norbert Schmidt* might be able to get XX-NS 1234).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In 2004 in [Nuremberg](/wiki/Nuremberg "Nuremberg"), a car owner was refused a number plate beginning *N-PD* because of the connection to the political party [NPD](/wiki/National_Democratic_Party_of_Germany "National Democratic Party of Germany").[[32]](#cite_note-55) After the terror group [National Socialist Underground](/wiki/National_Socialist_Underground "National Socialist Underground") was uncovered in 2011, the city of [Nuremberg](/wiki/Nuremberg "Nuremberg") refused number plates beginning with *N-SU* and even abolished the respective plates on their own vehicles of *Stadtentwässerung und Umweltanalytik* (sewage and environmental department).[[33]](#cite_note-56)
In the 2010s, some districts started banning licence plates with the middle letters IS which resembled the [Islamic State](/wiki/Islamic_State_of_Iraq_and_the_Levant "Islamic State of Iraq and the Levant").[[34]](#cite_note-57) The [Herford](/wiki/Herford_(district) "Herford (district)") district registration office ceased issuing registration plates with the combination HF-Z in April 2022 to avoid connotations with the use of the letter "[Z](/wiki/Z_(military_symbol) "Z (military symbol)")" as a symbol for the [2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine](/wiki/2022_Russian_invasion_of_Ukraine "2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine").[[35]](#cite_note-58)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/af/Aken.jpg/250px-Aken.jpg)](/wiki/File:Aken.jpg)

Permitted: [AC](/wiki/Aachen "Aachen")-AB

The combinations [STA](/wiki/Starnberg_(district) "Starnberg (district)")-SI, [S](/wiki/Stuttgart "Stuttgart")-ED[[29]](#cite_note-Stuttgart-49)/[SE](/wiki/Segeberg "Segeberg")-D,[[36]](#cite_note-Segeberg-59) [HE](/wiki/Helmstedt_(district) "Helmstedt (district)")-IL/[HEI](/wiki/Dithmarschen "Dithmarschen")-L, [IZ](/wiki/Steinburg "Steinburg")-AN and [WAF](/wiki/Warendorf "Warendorf")-FE are also forbidden or discouraged, to avoid association with [Stasi](/wiki/Stasi "Stasi"), the *[Sozialistische Einheitspartei](/wiki/Socialist_Unity_Party_of_Germany "Socialist Unity Party of Germany")* of the GDR, the [Nazi salute](/wiki/Nazi_salute "Nazi salute"), NAZI backwards and the German word for weapon respectively. Other combinations affected are [BUL](/wiki/Burglengenfeld "Burglengenfeld")-LE (German derogative for police, roughly comparable to *pig*),[[37]](#cite_note-60) [MO](/wiki/Moers "Moers")-RD (German word for murder),[[38]](#cite_note-61) [TO](/wiki/Nordsachsen "Nordsachsen")-D (German word for death), [KI](/wiki/Kiel "Kiel")-LL (kill) and [SU](/wiki/Siegburg "Siegburg")-FF (boozing).[[39]](#cite_note-62)[[40]](#cite_note-63)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/83/SEX146_licence_plate_of_Kreis_Segeberg.JPG/250px-SEX146_licence_plate_of_Kreis_Segeberg.JPG)](/wiki/File:SEX146_licence_plate_of_Kreis_Segeberg.JPG)

Consensual: [SE](/wiki/Segeberg "Segeberg")-X

On the other hand, plates that would seem offensive in other circumstances have been allowed, such as the infamous acronym [AC-AB](/wiki/Acab "Acab"). Since the 1950s, morals and customs have certainly changed, and combinations which may have appeared indecent then do not raise an eyebrow now. Therefore, it is not a problem to get a licence plate with S-EX,[[29]](#cite_note-Stuttgart-49) SE-X or SE-XY,[[36]](#cite_note-Segeberg-59) for example.

### Reserved combinations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Germany&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Reserved combinations")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/11/Kfz-Kennzeichen_Polizei_Sachsen.jpg/250px-Kfz-Kennzeichen_Polizei_Sachsen.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kfz-Kennzeichen_Polizei_Sachsen.jpg)

Licence plate of a police car in Saxony

For quite different reasons, some districts hold certain letter combinations reserved. The Saxon capital [Dresden](/wiki/Dresden "Dresden") issues all **DD-Q** plates to the state police vehicles. Likewise, [Erfurt](/wiki/Erfurt "Erfurt") uses **EF-LP** for the police in Thuringia. [Munich](/wiki/Munich "Munich") and other Bavarian cities reserve certain combinations with *P* for the police units within their [authorities](/wiki/Bavarian_State_Police#Organization "Bavarian State Police"), such as **[M](/wiki/Munich "Munich")-PM**, **[N](/wiki/Nuremberg "Nuremberg")-PP** or **[RO](/wiki/Rosenheim "Rosenheim")-P**.[[41]](#cite_note-64) Cologne issues **K-TX** to taxis and **K-LN** to the city's own vehicles.[[28]](#cite_note-Köln-48) In various districts, firefighter vehicles will be issued the middle letters **FW** standing for *Feuerwehr*.[[42]](#cite_note-65) The combinations **B-FA** and **BN-AA** with any 4-digit numbers are issued by Berlin and Bonn, respectively, to embassy domestic staff without diplomatic status.[[43]](#cite_note-KW-Diplo-66)

### Bogus licence plates

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Germany&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Bogus licence plates")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/55/German_license_plate.svg/250px-German_license_plate.svg.png)](/wiki/File:German_license_plate.svg)

Fake number plate, seemingly from Munich but obviously not correct, due to the umlaut and the leading digit 0

Sometimes, e.g. in movie films, it may be necessary to show licence plates which do not really belong to any vehicle. The easiest way would be to create a fictional area code, such as *NN-XY 555*. In the 1980s TV series *[Der Fahnder](/wiki/Der_Fahnder "Der Fahnder")*,[[44]](#cite_note-67) the area code **G** was used for a fictional "large city" ([German](/wiki/German_language "German language"): ***G**roßstadt*) in the [Ruhrgebiet](/wiki/Ruhrgebiet "Ruhrgebiet") area. However, if the plot is supposed to take place in a defined town or region, the audience would expect cars to show codes of that area on their number plates. When James Bond was driving through Hamburg in *[Tomorrow Never Dies](/wiki/Tomorrow_Never_Dies "Tomorrow Never Dies")*, the obviously fake Berlin licence plates on his BMW were soon pointed out.[[45]](#cite_note-68)

In the time before 2000, it had also been possible to use number plates with bogus identifiers containing the letters B, F, G, I, O and Q, which at that time were not issued in the middle group. Meanwhile, however, all these letters can appear on a real licence plate. In order to state clearly that the plate shown is a fictive one, the crew could use an impossible identifier, such as an *umlaut* in this middle section. Yet another way would be to have a valid registration issued (or at least reserved) by the district authority.

## Registration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Germany&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Registration")]

### Procedures

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Germany&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Procedures")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/18/Schildershop.jpg/250px-Schildershop.jpg)](/wiki/File:Schildershop.jpg)

Several shops advertising *Schilder* (plates), in the street of the registration authority

Vehicles must be registered with their owner's name and current address. On proof of identity, vehicle documentation and liability insurance, the registration will take place in the [district](/wiki/Districts_of_Germany "Districts of Germany") authority competent for the respective address.[[5]](#cite_note-FZV-10): §6 An alphanumeric combination, which can be reserved according to personal wishes, will be issued to the vehicle. The physical plates, however, have to be acquired separately, either at a local store or online.[[46]](#cite_note-69) Both the dimensions of the plates and the typeface of letters and numbers are standardized. After purchasing the number plate, the official stamps must be applied, back at the registration office.[[5]](#cite_note-FZV-10): §12(3) A fee is payable for the registration, in addition to the expenses for the plates.

Any registration or change of it will be registered both within the district authority and nationwide. The latter task is carried out by the Central Vehicle Register (*Zentrales Fahrzeugregister*, ZFZR) which is controlled by the Federal Motor Transport Authority (*Kraftfahrt-Bundesamt*, KBA).[[11]](#cite_note-ZFZR-20): §31 The data which are stored there refer to the vehicle, the owner and the insurance.[[11]](#cite_note-ZFZR-20): §33[[47]](#cite_note-70) Inquiries from foreign authorities can be addressed to ZFZR which will then direct them to the correct district.[[11]](#cite_note-ZFZR-20): §37[[48]](#cite_note-71)

Changes, such as a vehicle being sold or its owner moving residence, must be registered to keep the vehicle documentation up to date.[[5]](#cite_note-FZV-10): §15 If the vehicle remains within the district, the licence plates may be retained. A vehicle being relocated outside of the district has to be registered at the authority now competent. Whilst it was mandatory to have the plates altered, according to the current address, this obligation was reduced in 2015 and has meanwhile been abolished. Since then, it is generally not possible any more to tell the owner's district of residence from the area code on the plates, as they may have registered it at a former residence e.g. in Hamburg yet moved to Frankfurt meanwhile.[[49]](#cite_note-72)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/25/Defacedgermanplate.JPG/250px-Defacedgermanplate.JPG)](/wiki/File:Defacedgermanplate.JPG)

Example of a defaced plate – notice how the bottom seal is completely gone, due to scraping. (from [Kronach](/wiki/Kronach "Kronach")).

When owners choose to deregister their vehicle, the officer at the local authority will want to see the licence plates with defaced seals on them as proof that the plate can no longer be used in public. For this purpose, scratching tools are available for use at the registration office. Once defaced, the plates may only be used legally on public roads for one return journey to the owner's residence. If a vehicle is to be deregistered and a new one registered to the same owner, it is possible to swap the licence plate from old to new within the same process. Documentation and fees are necessary nonetheless, and neither vehicle should be used to reach the authority, as the assignment of the number changes by the minute.

It is general practice for owners to deregister their vehicles when selling them, typically when a sale is agreed. A sales contract is highly recommended, and various forms are available online for free.[[50]](#cite_note-73) A seller may hand over their vehicle with valid licence plates and papers still in their name to the new owner, and the owner will complete the registration transfer to their name. In a scenario without a proper sales contract, the seller may become liable when the buyer commits traffic violations or even criminal acts related to the car or plates. It is generally not recommended to sell used cars with licence plates.

A car whose owner has not paid their insurance premium and is reported to the police by the insurance company may get *entstempelt*, *unstamped* when found in a public place. The police will remove the official seal using a scratching tool like a screwdriver, leaving the plate without a valid seal. This renders the vehicle illegal to be used, or to be left in a public place, unless the insurance premium is paid and plates are fitted with a new official seal. A one-time journey to the relevant registration authorities is permitted to have the seal reinstated, once insurance is restored.

### Costs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Germany&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Costs")]

As of 2020, the average registration fee is €26.00[[51]](#cite_note-74) whilst further fees may apply for choosing an individual identifier or for reservation of such. Whereas some of these amounts are ordained by federal laws, others vary slightly from one district to the next.[[52]](#cite_note-75) The prices for number plates, on the other hand, are subject to the free market and range from less than €10 up to around €40 per piece. Generally, it is cheaper to have the plates ordered online, but faster to walk across the street and have them made on the spot.

Further costs arise for [motor vehicle tax](/wiki/Road_tax#Germany "Road tax") (€194 on average[[53]](#cite_note-76) yet very much depending on engine and emissions) and mandatory liability insurance (€260 on average, in 2019;[[54]](#cite_note-77) depending on the model of the vehicle, age and residence of the owner, etc.). [Comprehensive insurance](/wiki/Vehicle_insurance#Germany "Vehicle insurance") is recommended but voluntary.

### Special types of registration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Germany&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Special types of registration")]

Besides the most common way of registering a vehicle for everyday, all-year use indefinitely, it is possible to register for several months of each year, or for a few days in order to export the vehicle abroad. As well it is allowed, under certain restrictions, to register two vehicles (such as a car and a [motorhome](/wiki/Motorhome "Motorhome")) under one number, with the same main licence plate. These variations may save expenses in tax and insurance. Further ways of saving apply to vintage cars and to electrically powered vehicles. Each of these special registrations are represented in the respective licence plate.

## Special codes, colours and formats

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Germany&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Special codes, colours and formats")]

Certain types of vehicle bear special codes.[[5]](#cite_note-FZV-10): App. 3[[r]](#cite_note-78)

### Different codes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Germany&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Different codes")]

In deviating from the system described above, vehicles registered to federal, state or communal owners can bear licence plates not showing the district and sometimes omitting the middle letters.

#### Highest state offices

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Germany&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Highest state offices")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bd/Licenseplate_of_limousine.png/250px-Licenseplate_of_limousine.png)](/wiki/File:Licenseplate_of_limousine.png)

Plate of the German Chancellor

The [President](/wiki/President_of_Germany "President of Germany") uses the licence plate **0-1**, the [Chancellor](/wiki/Chancellor_of_Germany_(Federal_Republic) "Chancellor of Germany (Federal Republic)") uses **0-2**, the [Foreign Minister](/wiki/Minister_for_Foreign_Affairs_(Germany) "Minister for Foreign Affairs (Germany)") uses **0-3** and the *First [State Secretary](/wiki/Secretary_of_State#Germany "Secretary of State") of the [Foreign Office](/wiki/Foreign_Office_(Germany) "Foreign Office (Germany)")* (i.e. the deputy Foreign Minister) uses **0-4**. The [President of the Parliament](/wiki/President_of_the_Bundestag "President of the Bundestag") uses **1-1**. This reflects the fact that the Parliament's president is not part of the executive branch but still ranks higher in (symbolic) importance than the Chancellor. These vehicles are tax-exempt and need not to be insured since the German government acts as insurer.

#### Federal government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Germany&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Federal government")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/25/Bundesfinanzverwaltung_license_plate_of_Germany.jpg/250px-Bundesfinanzverwaltung_license_plate_of_Germany.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesfinanzverwaltung_license_plate_of_Germany.jpg)

*Bundesfinanzverwaltung* (customs)

Instead of a city code (which would mostly mean B for Berlin) the federal government uses an exclusive abbreviation of their own. The code **BD** *(Bundesrepublik Deutschland)* applies to the federal government, ministries, parliament, presidential office, etc. which are identified by their initial number.

#### Federal administration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Germany&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Federal administration")]

Some branches and institutions of the federal government use their special prefix (often an abbreviation of their name) instead of a city code.

* The *[Technisches Hilfswerk](/wiki/Technisches_Hilfswerk "Technisches Hilfswerk")* (German Federal Agency for Technical Relief) uses its abbreviation **THW**, so the plates read *THW-80000*, for example. All numbers on THW plates start either with the digit 8 or 9.
* The *Wasserstraßen- und Schifffahrtsverwaltung des Bundes* (Federal Administration of Waterways and Navigation) uses **BW** followed by a digit identifying the region of the office (from 1=north to 7=south).
* Before the *[Deutsche Bundesbahn](/wiki/Deutsche_Bundesbahn "Deutsche Bundesbahn")* (German Federal Railways) was corporatised and the *[Deutsche Bundespost](/wiki/Deutsche_Bundespost "Deutsche Bundespost")* (German Federal Mail) was privatised, they used the abbreviations **DB** and **BP** (e.g. *DB-12345*, *BP-12345*).
* The **[federal police](/wiki/German_Federal_Police "German Federal Police")** uses the code **BP** for *Bundespolizei* instead of a local code. Before 2006 the code **BG**, for their former name *[Bundesgrenzschutz](/wiki/Bundesgrenzschutz "Bundesgrenzschutz")*, was used in the scheme *BG-12345*. This old code still remains valid, but any new vehicles will get the new code BP.

#### State governments

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Germany&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: State governments")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/25/%28BWL%29_Baden-W%C3%BCrttemberg_Landesregierung_1%3DLandtag.jpg/250px-%28BWL%29_Baden-W%C3%BCrttemberg_Landesregierung_1%3DLandtag.jpg)](/wiki/File:(BWL)_Baden-W%C3%BCrttemberg_Landesregierung_1%3DLandtag.jpg)

Vehicle of [Baden-Württemberg](/wiki/Baden-W%C3%BCrttemberg "Baden-Württemberg") state government

In a similar way as the federal government, the [state](/wiki/States_of_Germany "States of Germany") governments and [diets](/wiki/Diet_(assembly) "Diet (assembly)") can use their respective codes. This difference is not made in the *[Stadtstaaten](/wiki/City-state#Non-sovereign_city-states "City-state")* Berlin, Hamburg and Bremen, as they fulfil both district and state function in addition to their municipality's. In some *Bundesländer*, such as [North Rhine-Westphalia](/wiki/North_Rhine-Westphalia "North Rhine-Westphalia"), the state code is also used by the [police](/wiki/North_Rhine-Westphalia_Police "North Rhine-Westphalia Police").[[example 7]](#cite_note-79)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/81/VW_Fridolin_Heusenstamm_05082011.JPG/250px-VW_Fridolin_Heusenstamm_05082011.JPG)](/wiki/File:VW_Fridolin_Heusenstamm_05082011.JPG)

#### Public sector

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Germany&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Public sector")]

Before the legal reforms of 2006, official vehicles such as police, fire fighting and municipal administration did not carry a letter after the sticker, but only the district prefix and a number, such as *M-1234*. These included:

* vehicles of the district government: 1–199, 1000–1999, 10000–19999
* vehicles of the local government, e.g. fire brigade: 200–299, 2000–2999, 20000–29999, 300–399
* police: 3000–3999, 7000–7999, 30000–39999, 70000–79999
* disaster relief (mostly changed to "THW", see above): 8000–8999, 80000–89999

This style of plate is no longer issued in most states, but many official vehicles which were registered before 2006 still carry this type of plate.

A similar style is issued by some districts to consular or diplomatic vehicles in the form Aaa-9NNn (example: D-921). Unlike the other style of diplomatic/consular plates issued in Berlin and Bonn *(see below)*, this plate does not indicate the nationality of the mission.

#### Diplomatic plates

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Germany&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Diplomatic plates")]

Plates of cars covered by [diplomatic immunity](/wiki/Diplomatic_immunity "Diplomatic immunity") bear the digit **0** (Zero), followed by a two- or three-digit number which indicates the specific diplomatic mission, a hyphen and another number counting within this mission. Traditionally, a digit **1** in this final place denotes the ambassador or *[chef de mission](/wiki/Chef_de_mission "Chef de mission")*. Lower-ranking embassy or consular staff without full diplomatic status are issued plates with the regular city code (mostly **B** for the capital, Berlin, or **BN** for the former capital, [Bonn](/wiki/Bonn "Bonn")); the following numerals are analogous to the "0-plates", e.g. **B 19–256**. Further holders of diplomatic plates are certain international organizations, such as the [UNHCR](/wiki/UNHCR "UNHCR") or the [European Central Bank](/wiki/European_Central_Bank "European Central Bank").[[43]](#cite_note-KW-Diplo-66)

#### Military

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Germany&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Military")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7a/Deutsches_Bundeswehr-Kfz-Kennzeichen.jpg/250px-Deutsches_Bundeswehr-Kfz-Kennzeichen.jpg)](/wiki/File:Deutsches_Bundeswehr-Kfz-Kennzeichen.jpg)

*Bundeswehr* (armed forces)

* [Bundeswehr](/wiki/Bundeswehr "Bundeswehr") uses old style non-reflecting plates. The German flag is shown, instead of the blue EU strip. Bundeswehr plates use the letter **Y** instead of a city code, as no German city has this initial. The Y is followed by a dash and a six-digit number (or five digits for motorcycles), for example **Y-123 456**. These vehicles are tax-exempt and need not to be insured since the German government acts as insurer. There is also no mandatory technical inspection required but the Armed Forces carry out a regular internal inspection on these vehicles similar to the official inspection.
* Military vehicles which are used by the [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO") headquarters in Germany use the same design as the Y-plates except they carry the letter **X** followed by a four-digit number, for example **X-1234**.

### Different colours

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Germany&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Different colours")]

Very rarely, German licence plates bear characters in different colours than black. These exceptions are:

#### Green on white

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Germany&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Green on white")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f8/Deutsches_Kfz-Kennzeichen_f%C3%BCr_steuerbefreite_Fahrzeuge_%28gr%C3%BCne_Schrift%29.jpg/250px-Deutsches_Kfz-Kennzeichen_f%C3%BCr_steuerbefreite_Fahrzeuge_%28gr%C3%BCne_Schrift%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Deutsches_Kfz-Kennzeichen_f%C3%BCr_steuerbefreite_Fahrzeuge_(gr%C3%BCne_Schrift).jpg)

Plate for tax-exempt vehicles

Vehicles which are exempt from vehicle taxes (for example [ambulances](/wiki/Ambulance "Ambulance"), [tractors](/wiki/Tractor "Tractor"), agricultural trailers, trailers for boats or trailers for gliders) have green print on a white background plate.[[5]](#cite_note-FZV-10): §10(2) Regular trailers for lorries can be exempted from tax if the owner agrees to pay an increased tax on the vehicle which tows the trailer.

#### Red on white – 06

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Germany&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Red on white – 06")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bb/Rotes_DIN-Kennzeichen_06.jpg/250px-Rotes_DIN-Kennzeichen_06.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rotes_DIN-Kennzeichen_06.jpg)

Plate for dealer's cars (red colour, old DIN-style)

[Car dealers](/wiki/Car_dealership "Car dealership")' plates are in red print on a white background, and the code begins with **06**.[[5]](#cite_note-FZV-10): §41 Red plates may be attached to cars which are changing hands, such as the test run of unregistered cars, and the [liability insurance](/wiki/Liability_insurance "Liability insurance") is connected to the plate, not a specific car.

#### Red on white – 07

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Germany&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Red on white – 07")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/41/Oldtimer_plate_Kreis_Stendal.JPG/250px-Oldtimer_plate_Kreis_Stendal.JPG)](/wiki/File:Oldtimer_plate_Kreis_Stendal.JPG)

Plate for vintage car collector

Red plates starting with the number 07 are reserved for collectors of vintage vehicles.[[5]](#cite_note-FZV-10): §43 Originally, vintage vehicles had a required minimum age of 20 years from first registering. Since April 2007, the required minimum age has been 30 years. Plates issued under the old 20 years rule remained valid after this date. The collectors must get an official certificate of approval (such as no criminal records). They are allowed to use one set of plates on any of their vehicles under the condition that they keep a strict record of use. No day-to-day use of the vehicles is allowed. A valid official technical inspection is not mandatory but the vehicles have to be technically fit for use on public roads.

### Different formats and styles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Germany&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Different formats and styles")]

#### H for historic

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Germany&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: H for historic")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9f/Deutsches_Kfz-Kennzeichen_f%C3%BCr_historische_Fahrzeuge_%28H-Kennzeichen%29.jpg/250px-Deutsches_Kfz-Kennzeichen_f%C3%BCr_historische_Fahrzeuge_%28H-Kennzeichen%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Deutsches_Kfz-Kennzeichen_f%C3%BCr_historische_Fahrzeuge_(H-Kennzeichen).jpg)

Plate for a specific vintage car

[Classic vehicles](/wiki/Classic_car "Classic car") (known in German by the [pseudo-English](/wiki/Pseudo-English "Pseudo-English") expression *Oldtimer*) can get an **H** (*historisch*, [historic](/wiki/Antique_vehicle_registration "Antique vehicle registration")) at the end of the plate, such as *[K](/wiki/Cologne "Cologne")-AA 100H* in order to preserve the so-called "vehicle of cultural value" (*kraftfahrtechnisches Kulturgut*).[[5]](#cite_note-FZV-10): §10(1) This also implies a flat tax of around €190 per year. It is popular to choose the digits so that they indicate the car's year of manufacture. The requirements for a vehicle to receive an H-Plate are:

* The first documented registration was at least 30 years ago.
* The car must be in mostly original and preservation worthy condition. Preservation worthy means a grade C by popular car grading standards. The older the car, the more signs of usage it can show. This purely concerns the car's appearance; the road worthiness is established by separate mandatory safety inspections.[[s]](#cite_note-82)

#### E for electric

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Germany&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: E for electric")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/55/German_electric_car_license_plate.jpg/250px-German_electric_car_license_plate.jpg)](/wiki/File:German_electric_car_license_plate.jpg)

Plate for Plug-in electric vehicle

The 2015 Electric Mobility Act authorised issuing special licence plates for [plug-in electric vehicles](/wiki/Plug-in_electric_vehicle "Plug-in electric vehicle") to allow proper identification to avoid abuses of these privileges.[[5]](#cite_note-FZV-10): §11[[57]](#cite_note-83)[[58]](#cite_note-84) The special licence plate adds the letter **E** at the end of the licence number. Owners of [all-electric cars](/wiki/All-electric_car "All-electric car") and [plug-in hybrids](/wiki/Plug-in_hybrid "Plug-in hybrid") with a minimum all-electric range of 40 km (25 mi) can apply for the special licence. The minimum range for eligible plug-in hybrids was increased from 30 km (19 mi) from 1 January 2018.[[59]](#cite_note-85)[[60]](#cite_note-86)

#### Seasonal

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Germany&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Seasonal")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/86/FRIES26.JPG/250px-FRIES26.JPG)](/wiki/File:FRIES26.JPG)

Seasonal number plate, *here* valid from 1 April to 31 October of each year

Seasonal number plates are popular for motorbikes or convertibles in the summertime, or for "winter cars" substituting them, yet these plates are available for any vehicle.[[5]](#cite_note-FZV-10): §10(3) They bear two 2-digit numbers at the end of the plate indicating the months between which they are registered to drive, with the licence being valid from the start of the upper month until the end of the lower month. This results in lower car taxes, as well as lower insurance premiums.

#### Interchangeable

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Germany&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Interchangeable")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/88/Special_license_plate_Germany_Berlin.JPG/250px-Special_license_plate_Germany_Berlin.JPG)](/wiki/File:Special_license_plate_Germany_Berlin.JPG)

Interchangeable licence plate (*Wechselkennzeichen*)

Two vehicles of the same class (two cars, two motorbikes or light vehicles, two trailers) may be registered with an alternating licence plate.[[5]](#cite_note-FZV-10): §9(2) In this case, only the last digit varies – e.g. **B-KJ 414|5** and **B-KJ 414|6** – and is printed on an extra plate which remains on either vehicle, whereas the main part of the plate has to be attached to the vehicle in use. The main part carries the registration seal and a small letter W for *Wechselkennzeichen*, the individual part carries the technical inspection seal and below, in very small letters, the associated main number. Both vehicles have to pay full tax, yet the insurance premium may be discounted.[[61]](#cite_note-87)

#### Temporary registration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Germany&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Temporary registration")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/75/Deutsches_Kurzzeit-KFZ-Kennzeichen.jpg/250px-Deutsches_Kurzzeit-KFZ-Kennzeichen.jpg)](/wiki/File:Deutsches_Kurzzeit-KFZ-Kennzeichen.jpg)

Temporary plate (*Kurzzeit-Kennzeichen*); this one was valid until 9 March 2004.

Used vehicles which are not currently registered to any person or company – or have been deregistered by their current owner, temporarily or permanently – can be driven on public roads using short-term plates, valid for five days only.[[5]](#cite_note-FZV-10): §42 These are known as "temporary number plates" or "yellow number plates", due to the yellow stripe. The first letter(s) indicate the issuing authority, as in regular German registrations. The numeric code starts with the numbers **04**, e.g. *DD-04321*, and the plate has a yellow stripe on the right showing when they are valid. The date is listed numerically, on three lines, reading day, month, year, with two digits each. The vehicle need not have a valid technical inspection, however it must be technically fit to be operated in public. Typically they are used to drive to/from a technical inspection, or to move storage location of the vehicle. Insurance premiums are quite high, appr. €100 for the above-mentioned 5 days. Most insurance companies credit this premium if the car is registered as a normal car with the same insurance company after these 5 days. These temporary plates are only valid within Germany, and cannot be used to export the vehicle to a foreign country nor for transit. They can only be obtained by a resident of Germany.

#### Export

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Germany&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Export")]

*Ausfuhrkennzeichen* (export plates) are used for exporting vehicles abroad.[[5]](#cite_note-FZV-10): §45 The owner does not have to be a German resident to register the car, but must provide identification such as a passport or ID card. The date on the red stripe on the right hand side shows the expiration date of the plate, as it indicates for how long the vehicle insurance and tax are paid. After this date, the vehicle must have left Germany, and is automatically deregistered from the German vehicle registration system. Use of the vehicle within Germany is permitted until the export date.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Germany&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: History")]

### German Empire and Weimar Republic

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Germany&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: German Empire and Weimar Republic")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/58/Kennzeichen_Th%C3%BCringen_Deutschland_1930s.jpg/250px-Kennzeichen_Th%C3%BCringen_Deutschland_1930s.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kennzeichen_Th%C3%BCringen_Deutschland_1930s.jpg)

Licence plate from Thuringia (1930s)

The first German licence plates that had a lettering plan were issued from 1906 onwards. The various states and realms which made up the [German Empire](/wiki/German_Empire "German Empire") used different prefixes, such as [Roman numerals](/wiki/Roman_numerals "Roman numerals") (I representing Prussia, II Bavaria, III Württemberg, etc.) or plain letters (HH for Hansestadt Hamburg, e.g.). Larger states added further identifiers for their provinces or regions. Saxony did not use any statewide numeral and only used Roman numerals for its provinces.[[62]](#cite_note-88)[[63]](#cite_note-89)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0b/Karte_Deutsches_Reich%2C_Gliederung_der_oberen_Verwaltungsebenen_1900-01-01.png/500px-Karte_Deutsches_Reich%2C_Gliederung_der_oberen_Verwaltungsebenen_1900-01-01.png)](/wiki/File:Karte_Deutsches_Reich,_Gliederung_der_oberen_Verwaltungsebenen_1900-01-01.png)

During World War I the German Army was assigned the combination MK for *Militärkraftwagen des Deutschen Heeres*, military vehicle of the German Army. After the war, during the [Weimar Republic](/wiki/Weimar_Republic "Weimar Republic"), the German Army used RW for *[Reichswehr](/wiki/Reichswehr "Reichswehr")*. Beyond this, there were no significant changes after the overthrow of the German monarchy.

### Nazi Germany

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Germany&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: Nazi Germany")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c9/Volkswagen_K%C3%BCbelwagen_Kennzeichen_1940%E2%80%9345.jpg/250px-Volkswagen_K%C3%BCbelwagen_Kennzeichen_1940%E2%80%9345.jpg)](/wiki/File:Volkswagen_K%C3%BCbelwagen_Kennzeichen_1940%E2%80%9345.jpg)

Army vehicle

During the Nazi regime (1933–1945), the system of licence plates was basically continued. New combinations were issued for nationwide institutions or organisations, such as DR ([Deutsche Reichsbahn](/wiki/Deutsche_Reichsbahn "Deutsche Reichsbahn")) for the railway authority, WH *[Wehrmacht Heer](/wiki/German_Army_(Wehrmacht) "German Army (Wehrmacht)")*, WL *[Wehrmacht Luftwaffe](/wiki/Luftwaffe "Luftwaffe")*, WM *[Wehrmacht Kriegsmarine](/wiki/Kriegsmarine "Kriegsmarine")* and WT *Wehrmacht Straßentransportdienst* for the military, or POL for the police.

While the Nazi state expanded and waged war, their bureaucrats applied their systems, including licence plates, to occupied countries or territories. Thus, plates of similar style were introduced in Austria, on Czech and Polish territory, in Alsace and Lorraine, and beyond.

### Postwar Germany

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Germany&action=edit&section=45 "Edit section: Postwar Germany")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a8/Deutschland_Besatzungszonen_8_Jun_1947_-_22_Apr_1949.svg/250px-Deutschland_Besatzungszonen_8_Jun_1947_-_22_Apr_1949.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Deutschland_Besatzungszonen_8_Jun_1947_-_22_Apr_1949.svg)

Occupation zones of Germany, 8 Jun 1947 - 22 Apr 1949

After 1945, however, the victorious allied forces abolished the system of German licence plates and instead assigned new lettering combinations in their respective [occupation zones](/wiki/Allied_Occupation_Zones_in_Germany "Allied Occupation Zones in Germany"). Although each nation implemented their own ideas initially, a system for all four zones was introduced by 1949.[[64]](#cite_note-90) At first, the different zones were distinguishable by the first letter prefix **A, B, F** or **S** standing for the American, British, French or Soviet occupation zone, respectively. A second letter below indicated the area or country in question, such as **A**  
**B** stood for *American zone/Bavaria*. This area code was followed by a two-digit number signifying the district and another number counting within that area. When the numbers became scarce after some years, another zone prefix (e. g. without the first letter) would be introduced additionally.

The city of Berlin had a special status and, consequentially, special plates. Having abolished the old **I A** number plates in 1945, the Soviet occupation forces issued plates with [Cyrillic](/wiki/Cyrillic "Cyrillic") characters at first. Motorcycles were issued **БM** (=BM, 1945–1946) and **ГM** (GM, 1945–1947). Cars, lorries and buses received **ГФ** (=GF, 1945–1946) and **БГ** (=BG, 1945–1947).[[65]](#cite_note-91) These were replaced on the insistence of the western powers, first to **KB** for *Kommandatura Berlin* and, in the Eastern part of the city, to **GB** in 1948.

#### Vehicles of occupation/NATO forces

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Germany&action=edit&section=46 "Edit section: Vehicles of occupation/NATO forces")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6c/Germany_occupation_1947_license_plate_graphic.png/250px-Germany_occupation_1947_license_plate_graphic.png)](/wiki/File:Germany_occupation_1947_license_plate_graphic.png)

Occupation 1947 licence plate

The British Army of the Rhine (BAOR), initially occupation forces, later NATO elements, issued servicemen with plates carrying white letters and numerals on a black background for their personal vehicles. These cars stood out in comparison to the black on white German plates, and following the terrorist murder of a British serviceman[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*], identified when returning to his car with BAOR licence plates, servicemen had to opt for their cars to carry either UK plates (generally right-hand drive vehicles) or German plates (generally left-hand drive vehicles). During the time that Belgian forces were stationed in West Germany, white on black plates similar to the BAOR plates were used.

#### US Forces vehicles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Germany&action=edit&section=47 "Edit section: US Forces vehicles")]

Main article: [Vehicle registration plates of the United States Army in Germany](/wiki/Vehicle_registration_plates_of_the_United_States_Army_in_Germany "Vehicle registration plates of the United States Army in Germany")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c8/HK-Nummernschild_der_US-Streitkr%C3%A4fte.jpg/250px-HK-Nummernschild_der_US-Streitkr%C3%A4fte.jpg)](/wiki/File:HK-Nummernschild_der_US-Streitkr%C3%A4fte.jpg)

"HK" number plate

The American Forces have tried to "blend in" their servicepersons′ private vehicles in another way. Starting in 2000, they adopted a type of license plates which closely resembled the German plates yet bore area codes which were at that time not assigned to any district, i.e. AD, AF and HK,[[u]](#cite_note-93) later also IF and QQ. These codes still stood out, especially as they bore the [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO") symbol instead of the EU's circle of stars and the registration seal candidly read *Streitkräfte der Vereinigten Staaten von Amerika* instead of, e.g. *Bayern, [Landratsamt Neustadt a.d. Waldnaab](/wiki/Neustadt_an_der_Waldnaab_(district) "Neustadt an der Waldnaab (district)")*. Since 2006, the vehicles in question bear license plates with regular German area codes, generally referring to the district of their official stationing.

### East Germany, *DDR*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Germany&action=edit&section=48 "Edit section: East Germany, DDR")]

Main article: [Vehicle registration plates of the German Democratic Republic](/wiki/Vehicle_registration_plates_of_the_German_Democratic_Republic "Vehicle registration plates of the German Democratic Republic")

The [German Democratic Republic](/wiki/German_Democratic_Republic "German Democratic Republic") issued their own style of licence plates beginning in 1953. The first letter would indicate the *Bezirk* ([administrative district](/wiki/Administrative_divisions_of_East_Germany "Administrative divisions of East Germany")[[v]](#cite_note-94)) where the vehicle was registered. These initials, however, did not refer to the name of the Bezirk but were distributed almost alphabetically from North to South.
