After German reunification in 1990, the DDR plates were soon abolished and the West German system introduced, starting in 1991 and completed in 1993. Even before this transition phase, it could be observed that licence plates in GDR scheme were produced with West German typeface on the respective machinery.

### West Germany

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Germany&action=edit&section=49 "Edit section: West Germany")]

In July 1956 the current system was introduced in then West Germany, replacing the post-war system.[[66]](#cite_note-95) The occupation zones were no longer referred to, instead the new system based on the [districts of Germany](/wiki/Districts_of_Germany "Districts of Germany"). Each of these was assigned an alphabetic code which had its origin in the name of the district, i.e. of the city or of the capital of the rural district. Quite often, a "district-free" city was surrounded by, or adjacent to a rural district of the same name. In this case, they would both share the code as well as the name, yet devise a way how to split the possible alphanumerical combinations.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/00/Numberplate_de_1985.jpg/250px-Numberplate_de_1985.jpg)](/wiki/File:Numberplate_de_1985.jpg)

Number plate in the 1956 style, from [Hannover](/wiki/Hannover "Hannover")

The number of letters in the area code hints at the size of the district. The basic idea was to even out the number of characters on all licence plates, because the most populous districts would have more cars and, consequently, more digits after the prefix. The largest German cities generally only have one-letter codes, such as B=[Berlin](/wiki/Berlin "Berlin"), M=[Munich](/wiki/Munich "Munich") (*München*), K=[Cologne](/wiki/Cologne "Cologne") (*Köln*), F=[Frankfurt](/wiki/Frankfurt "Frankfurt"), S=[Stuttgart](/wiki/Stuttgart "Stuttgart"), H=[Hannover](/wiki/Hannover "Hannover"). Therefore, cities or districts with fewer letters are generally assumed to be bigger and more important whereas three-letter codes tend to be regarded as rural and dull. Reflecting that, most districts aimed for a combination with fewer letters for their prefix code.

The most significant exception of the one-letter code is Germany's second largest city [Hamburg](/wiki/Hamburg "Hamburg") which bears HH for *Hansestadt Hamburg*, because of its historical membership in the [Hanseatic League](/wiki/Hanseatic_League "Hanseatic League"), reflected already in its prefix used between 1906 and 1945. A similar principle applies to [Bremen](/wiki/Bremen "Bremen") and [Bremerhaven](/wiki/Bremerhaven "Bremerhaven"), forming the state [Free Hanseatic City of Bremen](/wiki/Bremen_(state) "Bremen (state)") and sharing the common prefix HB (1906–1947, and again since 1956). Likewise, *Hansestadt* [Lübeck](/wiki/L%C3%BCbeck "Lübeck") received its former prefix HL, already used between 1906 and 1937 when its statehood was abolished.

The first drafts, however, had to be altered in a few cases. The district of [Wittlich](/wiki/Wittlich "Wittlich") rejected the code *WC*, understandably, and received **WIL** instead. The code *KZ*, initially projected for [Konstanz](/wiki/Konstanz_(district) "Konstanz (district)"), was withdrawn fast, due to recent history, and replaced by **KN**. Neither were *SA*, *SS* or *HJ* considered to be issued. The code *SD* was projected for [Stade](/wiki/Stade_(district) "Stade (district)") and was finally altered into **STD** after protests from that district who did not want to bear the abbreviation of the *[Sicherheitsdienst](/wiki/Sicherheitsdienst "Sicherheitsdienst")*.

When originally planned, the system included [codes for districts in Eastern Germany](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ostzonenverzeichnis_der_deutschen_Kfz-Kennzeichen "de:Ostzonenverzeichnis der deutschen Kfz-Kennzeichen") which were to be reserved until [reunification](/wiki/German_reunification "German reunification"). That included the territory of the [GDR](/wiki/German_Democratic_Republic "German Democratic Republic") as well as the territories [annexed](/wiki/Oder-Neisse_line "Oder-Neisse line") to [Poland](/wiki/Poland "Poland") and the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") after [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), which West Germany's government still claimed in that era until about 1970. When reunification came in 1990, the reserved codes (e.g. *P* for [Potsdam](/wiki/Potsdam "Potsdam")) were indeed issued to East German districts in January 1991, often as originally planned and as they existed at that time.

#### First changes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Germany&action=edit&section=50 "Edit section: First changes")]

Starting in the early 1970s, West German districts were extensively rearranged.[[67]](#cite_note-96) In order to reduce their number and so simplify governance, different steps could be taken:

* city districts "swallowed" neighboring municipalities and thus grew.[[example 8]](#cite_note-97)
* city districts lost their sovereignty and were integrated into the surrounding or neighbouring rural district.[[example 9]](#cite_note-98)
* rural districts merged with one or several others, or were split up between neighbouring districts.[[example 10]](#cite_note-99)
* single municipalities were moved between districts, as was deemed appropriate or practical.

In each of these cases, the new districts had to be endowed with an area code. Again, various solutions were possible:

* the largest or most populous district bestowed its name and code upon the newly created unit.[[example 11]](#cite_note-100)
* one former district gave its name and/or capital while another's area code was used for the new district.[[example 12]](#cite_note-101)
* the new unit was given a new name yet continued to use an existing area code.[[example 13]](#cite_note-102)
* the new unit was given a new name and created a new area code.[[example 14]](#cite_note-103)

In any case, the adamant rule was that *one* area code per district was valid and would be issued to any vehicle registered henceforth. Existing registrations would remain valid until the vehicle was removed from this district to be either relocated or permanently deregistered. Another rule, however, was abolished. Whereas rural districts had generally been named after their capital town, it was now possible to create new names, applying to geographical[[example 15]](#cite_note-104) or historical features.[[example 16]](#cite_note-105) As well it was possible to combine the names of the districts that had merged, either keeping one of their codes[[example 17]](#cite_note-106) or creating a new one.[[example 18]](#cite_note-107)

### Germany reunited

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Germany&action=edit&section=51 "Edit section: Germany reunited")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d3/Trabant_%287909447336%29.jpg/250px-Trabant_%287909447336%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Trabant_(7909447336).jpg)

Trabant registered in Stendal; pre-1994 typeface[[w]](#cite_note-108)

When the GDR ceased to exist and Germany was reunited in its present size on 3 October 1990, new area codes were issued to the East German districts. In many cases they could be taken from the old lists that had been prepared before 1956: **P** stood for [Potsdam](/wiki/Potsdam "Potsdam"), **EF** for [Erfurt](/wiki/Erfurt "Erfurt"), **SON** for [Sonneberg](/wiki/Sonneberg "Sonneberg"). Yet, a considerable number of codes was altered, either because a code which had been reserved for a district in today's Poland or Russia had become available,[[example 19]](#cite_note-109) or because the projected code had meanwhile been issued to a West German district.[[example 20]](#cite_note-110)

A prominent example of a reserved code being reused before reunification was one-letter **L** which was originally planned for [Leipzig](/wiki/Leipzig "Leipzig"), by far the largest German city starting with L. However this code was given to the newly formed [Hessian](/wiki/Hesse "Hesse") city of [Lahn](/wiki/Lahn,_Hesse "Lahn, Hesse") and the district [Lahn-Dill-Kreis](/wiki/Lahn-Dill-Kreis "Lahn-Dill-Kreis") in 1977, as hopes for reunification faded away. After the rather unexpected reunification (and Lahn city having split up again and thus abolished in 1979), the L was returned to the city of Leipzig and Lahn-Dill-Kreis was issued **LDK** instead.

The letter **G** was first reserved for the East German city of [Görlitz](/wiki/G%C3%B6rlitz "Görlitz") and later awarded to the city of [Gera](/wiki/Gera "Gera"), although both are smaller than the West German [Gelsenkirchen](/wiki/Gelsenkirchen "Gelsenkirchen") (**GE**). The area code **ZK** had been reserved, in the 1950s, for the city of [Zwickau](/wiki/Zwickau "Zwickau") but was rejected as ZK had become the abbreviation of the loathed *[Zentralkomitee](/wiki/Socialist_Unity_Party_of_Germany#Central_Committee "Socialist Unity Party of Germany")* of the former Communist party SED.

In analogy to the three northwestern *Hansestädte* Hamburg, Bremen and Lübeck, but without historical examples of formerly issued prefixes, four northeastern Hanseatic cities, [Greifswald](/wiki/Greifswald "Greifswald"), [Rostock](/wiki/Rostock "Rostock"), [Stralsund](/wiki/Stralsund "Stralsund") and [Wismar](/wiki/Wismar "Wismar"), chose the prefixes **HGW, HRO, HST** and **HWI**. There were no suitable two-letter codes available since **HG, HR, HS** and **HW** were already taken by West German districts.[[x]](#cite_note-111)

Beginning in the mid-1990s, however, districts in East Germany were rearranged again, similar to the West two decades before. Thus many of these codes issued before were now outdated, but could still be seen alongside the new code.[[example 21]](#cite_note-112) This rearrangement was continued in a second step after 2000, which created large districts with a remarkable variety of possible area codes registered. Still, only one of these was the current one which would be issued to vehicles registered at the moment.

### Liberalized registration rules

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Germany&action=edit&section=52 "Edit section: Liberalized registration rules")]

After the reorganization of districts, from the 1970s onward, many area codes expired and new ones were created at that time. However, number plates issued before these rearrangements remain valid, providing the vehicle is still in use and has not been reregistered since. So it was still possible, if rare, to see a [classic car](/wiki/Classic_car "Classic car") with registration codes of administrative units that have not existed for over 30 years.[[example 22]](#cite_note-113)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/62/Germany_2020_Ueberlingen_license_plate.jpg/250px-Germany_2020_Ueberlingen_license_plate.jpg)](/wiki/File:Germany_2020_Ueberlingen_license_plate.jpg)

Überlingen licence plate, reintroduced in [Bodenseekreis](/wiki/Bodenseekreis "Bodenseekreis") in 2020

A study conducted in 2010–12 produced the result that 72% of the German population would welcome the possibility to use again these abolished area codes whereas only 13% opposed the idea.[[12]](#cite_note-IKL-21)[[68]](#cite_note-114)
It was regarded as remarkable that even young people who had never driven a car with such an "old" prefix favoured the idea of this so-called *Kennzeichenliberalisierung* (licence plate liberalization).[[69]](#cite_note-115) For one, their chances at receiving a personalized license plate would improve if the aspired combination was available not once but several times within their district. Yet the main reason mentioned was a pride in their home town and their own roots, in times of globalization.[[70]](#cite_note-116)

The police, however, warned against introducing further codes, as it had turned out that observant citizens would easily notice a car with a number plate from a distant district, thus assisting the police in solving crimes. Plates from the vicinity, on the other hand, would be easier to remember in full, and this would also help to find offenders. More opposition came from local politicians who maintained they had at last succeeded in unifying their merged districts and healed the wounds of those inhabitants who had to give up "their" prefix. If that prefix was available again, they feared, it might lead to old feuds within districts flaming up anew[[71]](#cite_note-117) – or, as the district of [Westerwaldkreis](/wiki/Westerwaldkreis "Westerwaldkreis") put it, "reopen the old trenches between former Oberwesterwaldkreis (WEB) and former Unterwesterwaldkreis (MT)."[[72]](#cite_note-118)

Nonetheless, the [Federal Ministry of Transport](/wiki/Federal_Ministry_of_Transport_and_Digital_Infrastructure "Federal Ministry of Transport and Digital Infrastructure") complied with the majority of citizens. Beginning in November 2012 in some districts, and meanwhile nationwide, most of these expired prefix codes have been reintroduced, e.g. in the district of [Wesel](/wiki/Wesel_(district) "Wesel (district)"), it has again become possible to register vehicles with **MO** as used for the former district of [Moers](/wiki/Moers "Moers") and **DIN** as used for the former district of [Dinslaken](/wiki/Dinslaken "Dinslaken"), additionally to the standard **WES** which had been the only code issued since 1975. As of September 2024, the liberalization has led to 328 previously abolished codes being reintroduced.[[12]](#cite_note-IKL-21)

In 2024, the same college which had initiated this movement suggested a second step. Every town above 20,000 inhabitants should be able to apply for their own code, even if they had never had one and were subordinate municipalities of a district.[[73]](#cite_note-119) The reactions by the political bodies responsible for an implementation of this idea were restrained but varied.[[74]](#cite_note-120)[[75]](#cite_note-121)

### Slackening of rules

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Germany&action=edit&section=53 "Edit section: Slackening of rules")]

Furthermore, it has become possible to "take one's number along", i.e. to keep a licence plate issued at the previous address after moving away from that district.[[76]](#cite_note-122) For that reason, the area code and the respective state seal on a licence plate do not necessarily mean that the vehicle's owner really lives there.

In 2023, the city of [Munich](/wiki/Munich "Munich") applied for a second code, as the remaining free combinations with **M**, which the city has to share with the [district of Munich](/wiki/Munich_(district) "Munich (district)"), were becoming scarce. The code **MUC**, which is also the IATA code for [Munich Airport](/wiki/Munich_Airport "Munich Airport"), was granted by the Federal government on 21 September.[[77]](#cite_note-123)

## Insurance plates

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Germany&action=edit&section=54 "Edit section: Insurance plates")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b6/Versicherungskennzeichen.jpg/250px-Versicherungskennzeichen.jpg)](/wiki/File:Versicherungskennzeichen.jpg)

Insurance plates; the colour of the letters alternates yearly.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f9/German_microcar_-_right.jpg/250px-German_microcar_-_right.jpg)](/wiki/File:German_microcar_-_right.jpg)

Car with maximum speed reduced to 25 km/h (16 mph), hence using an insurance plate[[y]](#cite_note-124)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4d/E-Scooter_HL_Germany.jpg/250px-E-Scooter_HL_Germany.jpg)](/wiki/File:E-Scooter_HL_Germany.jpg)

E-Scooter

**Light motorised vehicles** such as mopeds, motorized wheelchairs and other small, low-power vehicles (such as vehicles for the physically handicapped, with a maximum speed of 50 km/h (31 mph)) are required to have a registration plate of a different kind. This *Versicherungskennzeichen* (insurance plate) uses a system of three digits on the top and three letters beneath.[[5]](#cite_note-FZV-10): §52 Both numbers and letters are chosen randomly so personalising the plates is not possible. Plates are much smaller[[z]](#cite_note-125) than the plates for normal cars and are only valid for one year from 1 March until the end of February the following year.[[78]](#cite_note-126) Those plates are sold by insurance companies, so the fee includes both the registration and the cost of one year's insurance for the vehicle. There are four colours used: black, blue, green for normal plates, and red for temporary use, such as testing (very rare). The first three colours alternate annually in order to make it easy to see whether the vehicle has the correct plate and insurance.

### Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Germany&action=edit&section=57 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-5)** Front plate, post-2010 hence without sticker; coloured registration seal of Steinburg district
2. **[^](#cite_ref-6)** Front plate with emission test sticker (valid July 1994) and silver registration seal "Stadt Karlsruhe"
3. **[^](#cite_ref-7)** Used on a motorcycle, from [Osterode am Harz](/wiki/Osterode_(district) "Osterode (district)")
4. **[^](#cite_ref-8)** seasonal plate, valid March to October every year
5. **[^](#cite_ref-9)** from Hansestadt [Lübeck](/wiki/L%C3%BCbeck "Lübeck"), final letter H for *historic*
6. **[^](#cite_ref-11)** Literally, "Road-Traffic-Admission-Ordinance", cf. [German compounds](/wiki/German_compounds "German compounds")
7. **[^](#cite_ref-12)** As necessary e.g. on certain cars, such as the rear of a [Volkswagen Beetle](/wiki/Volkswagen_Beetle "Volkswagen Beetle")
8. **[^](#cite_ref-25)** The term "rural district" does not necessarily denote rural surroundings or landscape but rather refers to the fact that the municipalities belonging to a "rural" district lack certain administrative rights of an "urban" district, such as issuing registration plates. These rights are exercised by the district office.
9. **[^](#cite_ref-27)** This habit is uncommon in most other European countries but does appear on regular [Montenegrin](/wiki/Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Montenegro "Vehicle registration plates of Montenegro"), [Croatian](/wiki/Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Croatia "Vehicle registration plates of Croatia") and [Serbian](/wiki/Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Serbia "Vehicle registration plates of Serbia") (letters Đ, Č, Ć, Š and Ž), [Åland registration plates](/wiki/Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Finland "Vehicle registration plates of Finland") (letters Å, Ä and Ö), as well as on [Finnish](/wiki/Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Finland "Vehicle registration plates of Finland"), [Swedish](/wiki/Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Sweden#Personal_plates "Vehicle registration plates of Sweden") (letters Å, Ä and Ö) and [Danish](/wiki/Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Denmark "Vehicle registration plates of Denmark") (letters Æ, Ø and Å) personal registration plates.
10. **[^](#cite_ref-28)** The letter ß does not occur in the beginning of any word and therefore did not have an upper-case form before 2008.
11. **[^](#cite_ref-29)** It may be argued that "there has to be a principle that two vehicles can't share the same letter-number combination, even if disregarding the dash (shown as blank on plates). So if a car has **SR-A 123**, no car can have **S-RA 123**. Otherwise this would violate the [Vienna Convention on Road Traffic](/wiki/Vienna_Convention_on_Road_Traffic "Vienna Convention on Road Traffic") annex 2 which says that letters and numerals are used for registration numbers, but not that spaces or dashes are significant." – However this principle has not been revealed yet, cf. discussion.
12. **[^](#cite_ref-40)** Note that the numbers run in the opposite direction as now, and the black mark 11–12–1 has not yet been invented.
13. **[^](#cite_ref-41)** The point behind figures 6 and 9 was meant to avoid confusion (**6.** vs **9.**).
14. **[^](#cite_ref-44)** There has to exist a method[*[which?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Avoid_weasel_words "Wikipedia:Avoid weasel words")*] to avoid two vehicles getting plate numbers only distinguished by the position of the blank space, because the [Vienna Convention on Road Traffic](/wiki/Vienna_Convention_on_Road_Traffic "Vienna Convention on Road Traffic") describes some rules about international road traffic, and it does not define blank or hyphen as a significant character on plates.[[24]](#cite_note-43)
15. **[^](#cite_ref-50)** TOD would have seemed indecent too, as it means *death*.
16. **[^](#cite_ref-51)** AH would rather be regarded as an abbreviation for *Altenheim* (old people's home) or *Autohaus* (car dealership); cf. [German Wikipedia](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/AH "de:AH").
17. **[^](#cite_ref-52)** It was certainly not common to refer to the Nazi *"Führer"* by his initials, neither before nor after 1945.
18. **[^](#cite_ref-78)** Appendix 3 defines the unique prefixes for vehicles of federal and state government bodies, agencies, police, the armed forces, diplomatic missions and privileged international organizations.
19. **[^](#cite_ref-82)** Universally accepted modifications include changes that benefit safety, such as seat belts and disc brakes, and environmental friendliness, such as catalytic converters and LPG conversions (if invisible from the outside). Further modifications that are generally accepted are those contemporary of the car's first registration (plus and minus 10 years, burden of proof lies with the owner through historic material such as photographs) and new paintjobs of any colour, including two-tone paint if originally offered and historic company logos, but no murals or custom patterns.
20. **[^](#cite_ref-92)** Saxony was abbreviated L for Leipzig, to avoid infamous SS.
21. **[^](#cite_ref-93)** HK has meanwhile been assigned to [Heidekreis](/wiki/Heidekreis "Heidekreis").
22. **[^](#cite_ref-94)** Any GDR *Bezirk* consisted of several *Kreise* and was much larger than the unit described as district elsewhere on this page.
23. **[^](#cite_ref-108)** With emission test sticker valid February 2011
24. **[^](#cite_ref-111)** **HG** = [Hochtaunuskreis](/wiki/Hochtaunuskreis "Hochtaunuskreis"), capital: [Bad Homburg vor der Höhe](/wiki/Bad_Homburg_vor_der_H%C3%B6he "Bad Homburg vor der Höhe")  
    **HR** = [Schwalm-Eder-Kreis](/wiki/Schwalm-Eder-Kreis "Schwalm-Eder-Kreis"), capital: [Homberg](/wiki/Homberg_(Efze) "Homberg (Efze)")  
    **HS** = [Kreis Heinsberg](/wiki/Kreis_Heinsberg "Kreis Heinsberg")  
    **HW** = Kreis [Halle (Westfalen)](/wiki/Halle_(Westfalen) "Halle (Westfalen)").
25. **[^](#cite_ref-124)** Additionally, the white sticker reading "25" signals low speed to following drivers.
26. **[^](#cite_ref-125)** Regular size 130 mm × 101 mm (5+1⁄8 in × 4 in); also available as sticker for [e-scooters](/wiki/E-scooters "E-scooters") in 67 mm × 55 mm (2+5⁄8 in × 2+1⁄8 in)

### Examples

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Germany&action=edit&section=58 "Edit section: Examples")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-18)** When the districts of Eutin and Oldenburg in Holstein merged to form the new district of [Ostholstein](/wiki/Ostholstein "Ostholstein"), in 1970, both their codes EUT and OLD became obsolete and were replaced by OH.
2. **[^](#cite_ref-19)**  The urban district of [Straubing](/wiki/Straubing "Straubing") has had one letter after the area code (**SR-A 123**), whereas the surrounding district [Straubing-Bogen](/wiki/Straubing-Bogen "Straubing-Bogen") has two letters (**SR-AB 123**) in this place.
3. **[^](#cite_ref-23)** Up to 9 in [Mecklenburgische Seenplatte (district)](/wiki/Mecklenburgische_Seenplatte_(district) "Mecklenburgische Seenplatte (district)"): MSE, AT, DM, MC, MST, MÜR, NZ, RM, WRN
4. **[^](#cite_ref-24)** ESB, formerly standing for Eschenbach district, is now issued by the districts of Neustadt a.d. Waldnaab, Amberg-Sulzbach, Bayreuth and Nürnberger Land.
5. **[^](#cite_ref-30)** Drivers from [Offenbach](/wiki/Offenbach_am_Main "Offenbach am Main") are slandered to be *ohne Verstand* (without sense) and even misspell it *OF*.
6. **[^](#cite_ref-42)** In [Nürnberger Land](/wiki/N%C3%BCrnberger_Land "Nürnberger Land") district e.g., LAU and HEB can be used in any combination, ESB with N only, PEG with A only, N with one-letter identifiers only, yet excluding the "new" letters B, F, G, I, O, Q and S.
7. **[^](#cite_ref-79)** The Police of North Rhine-Westphalia uses *NRW 4*, *NRW 5* and, for motorbikes, *NRW 6* instead of a local code. This is followed by a four-digit number (e.g. NRW 4-1960).
8. **[^](#cite_ref-97)** The city of [Bielefeld](/wiki/Bielefeld "Bielefeld") incorporated the largest part of the eponymous rural district, almost doubling their population.
9. **[^](#cite_ref-98)** The district-free towns of [Marktredwitz](/wiki/Marktredwitz "Marktredwitz") and [Selb](/wiki/Selb "Selb") were merged into the district of [Wunsiedel](/wiki/Wunsiedel_(district) "Wunsiedel (district)").
10. **[^](#cite_ref-99)** The district of [Springe](/wiki/Springe "Springe") split up into the districts of [Hameln-Pyrmont](/wiki/Hameln-Pyrmont "Hameln-Pyrmont"), [Hanover](/wiki/Hanover_region "Hanover region"), [Hildesheim](/wiki/Hildesheim_(district) "Hildesheim (district)") and [Schaumburg](/wiki/Schaumburg "Schaumburg").
11. **[^](#cite_ref-100)** The urban district of [Schwandorf](/wiki/Schwandorf "Schwandorf") (SAD) merged with the rural districts of Burglengenfeld (BUL), Nabburg (NAB), Neunburg (NEN) and Oberviechtach (OVI) into a new rural [district of Schwandorf](/wiki/Schwandorf_(district) "Schwandorf (district)") (SAD).
12. **[^](#cite_ref-101)** The Rheingaukreis (RÜD for Rüdesheim) and Untertaunuskreis (SWA for Bad Schwalbach) districts merged into [Rheingau-Taunus-Kreis](/wiki/Rheingau-Taunus-Kreis "Rheingau-Taunus-Kreis"), choosing the area code RÜD but the capital Bad Schwalbach.
13. **[^](#cite_ref-102)** The districts of Eschwege and Witzenhausen merged into [Werra-Meißner-Kreis](/wiki/Werra-Mei%C3%9Fner-Kreis "Werra-Meißner-Kreis"), using Eschwege's ESW and dropping WIZ.
14. **[^](#cite_ref-103)** The districts of Eiderstedt (TÖN for [Tönning](/wiki/T%C3%B6nning "Tönning")), Husum (HUS) and Südtondern (NIB for [Niebüll](/wiki/Nieb%C3%BCll "Niebüll")) merged into [Nordfriesland](/wiki/Nordfriesland_(district) "Nordfriesland (district)") (NF).
15. **[^](#cite_ref-104)** The rural districts of Heidelberg (HD), Mannheim (MA) and part of Sinsheim (SNH) merged into [Rhein-Neckar-Kreis](/wiki/Rhein-Neckar-Kreis "Rhein-Neckar-Kreis") (HD); the districts of Alsfeld (ALS) and Lauterbach (LAT) merged into [Vogelsbergkreis](/wiki/Vogelsbergkreis "Vogelsbergkreis") (VB).
16. **[^](#cite_ref-105)** Öhringen (ÖHR) and Künzelsau (KÜN) merged into [Hohenlohekreis](/wiki/Hohenlohe_(district) "Hohenlohe (district)") (KÜN).
17. **[^](#cite_ref-106)** The rural districts of Straubing and Bogen merged to [Straubing-Bogen](/wiki/Straubing-Bogen "Straubing-Bogen"), keeping SR (and sharing it with [Straubing](/wiki/Straubing "Straubing") city district) while dropping Bogen's BOG.
18. **[^](#cite_ref-107)** The rural districts of Koblenz and Mayen merged to [Mayen-Koblenz](/wiki/Mayen-Koblenz "Mayen-Koblenz"), newly introducing MYK while dropping MY and leaving KO to the city of [Koblenz](/wiki/Koblenz "Koblenz").
19. **[^](#cite_ref-109)** DZ had once been reserved for [Danzig](/wiki/Gda%C5%84sk "Gdańsk") but was now issued to [Delitzsch](/wiki/Delitzsch "Delitzsch").
20. **[^](#cite_ref-110)** [Gotha](/wiki/Gotha "Gotha") had been scheduled to receive GT, yet this code went to the newly-created district of [Gütersloh](/wiki/G%C3%BCtersloh_(district)#History "Gütersloh (district)") in 1973. [Gotha](/wiki/Gotha_(district) "Gotha (district)") was given GTH instead.
21. **[^](#cite_ref-112)** In [Torgau-Oschatz](/wiki/Torgau-Oschatz "Torgau-Oschatz") district, merged in 1994, you might well see the new TO alongside Torgau's TG and Oschatz's OZ, at the same time.
22. **[^](#cite_ref-113)** EIN = [Einbeck](/wiki/Einbeck "Einbeck"), which was merged into [Northeim](/wiki/Northeim_(district) "Northeim (district)") in 1974.
